speaker: interviewer
text: --Basner. All right, could you tell me a little bit more about life in the small ghetto? How long you where there, altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: The small ghetto existed from-- from December, 1941, til-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --October, the beginning of October. I think the 2nd of October, 1943. So I was-- part of the time I was in the ghetto. Part of the time I was in Cousin [INAUDIBLE] place. Life in ghetto, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's hard to describe the life, what the people felt, and how life was. Because you couldn't see-- you couldn't see the-- the-- the finish line. When you are sitting in a jail or you have a sentence, you know then it'll take a year, or 10 years, or 15 years, and then you'll be free. There you couldn't see it. You didn't know when, what will happen. You didn't know what will happen the next minute. You were in their hands. In your-- German-- German, those hands. And they were very savage people, savage tyrants. And so for some people you-- you had to hope. And hope, it was one of the reasons who hold you alive, who saved your life. Losing your hope, it was losing your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people worked. They're hungry, they suffered, and they hoped. And there were going on some resistance movements in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were inside police. And there were-- all of them were young men, strong men, 20, 25. Most of them were from the Zionist organizations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they organized a resistance. But they were caught. The Germans learned about it. We don't know till yet, what-- what happened, what was the reason. But they were caught, and they were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish police who had organized--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: --into a resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Jewish police who had to take care of the order inside the ghetto. Not only the police, there were other people who participate in the resistance movement, many of them. But the police [? owe ?] the police. There were more than 40 people, all of them were killed in one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they trying to do something?

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They collected-- they didn't do nothing yet. They collected weapons, prepared it. But it didn't come, the moment when they could use it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were some of the strongest young men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So life was-- life was terrible. But it was still a ghetto. People would come home. They would go from house to house. You could move from one house to the other. It wasn't many-- much time, but anyway, you could meet somebody. You could talk and exchange. Bart--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barter some-- some stuff, what you-- you-- if you have, you know, you brought something and you could barter for other stuff. So-- so it was going on, the life in the  ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you practice Judaism at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Only if you were a believer you could, in the evenings, you could come together and people would observe. And when Passover came or other holy days, they would observe it in the evenings. They would come to some house and observe it. But officially, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any schools or newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There wasn't newspapers. There weren't schools. There weren't children. It was only schools were in the German ghetto, from Jewish who came from Germany. In our Ghetto, in the small ghetto, we didn't have children. So we didn't have schools. We have only a-- a hospital, where famous Jewish-- Jewish doctors would work. One of them, Professor [? Mintz, ?] one of the famous doctor, because he-- he performed a surgery on Lenin in Russia. And he was-- he saved him his life. And he was very famous. He worked in the Yiddish Bikkur Cholim. It was the Jewish hospital. And when he came to the ghetto, organized a hospital there. And he would perform, even there in such situations, such, he would perform some surgeries there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him? Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him later when-- I'll tell  you about life in Buchenwald. I was with him, together, and I saw him. I'll tell you what's happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you know about the German ghetto, and it-- what exactly was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German ghetto, what we-- after the liquidation of the big ghetto, after they took the 30,000, approximately, the Jewish women, children, older people, and some men, sure, to the forest of Rumbula. They brought people from Germany, transports from Germany and from other countries. From-- from Austria, from Czechoslovakia, they brought to Riga. The environment in Riga for them was very convenient. They could use the place and the people. They even bartered them and helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would bring the people. And most of the people-- not most. Biggest part of the people get killed immediately from the transport as it brought them out from Germany. And Germans, they make-- they will-- they like to show the people of Germany that they're taking them to other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made even, in movies, and showed them in their movies, you know, to show in the news that the people arrived and everything is OK with them. But in the same time, they would kill them [? exact ?] from-- from the freight-- freight cars. They would take them to the forest and kill them. But a little-- a small part of them was taken to the Riga ghetto, to the ghetto where-- in the homes where the Jewish people of this big ghetto live. And they came in. There was even food yet, on the table. Or-- or-- or everything was leaved as it was. And they came in, in the ghetto. There was a strange feeling between the Latvian Jewish and the German Jewish in that time. Because you felt that your people was taken away and they were brought-- brought their place. But we didn't know then, most of them were killed. And it was a very, very small part of them. And very few survived, you know, the German Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard about them. Did you have contact with the German Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the big ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in a separate ghetto. It called the German ghetto. After the police from the-- our ghetto, from the small ghetto were killed, the police of the German ghetto, the inside police, the Jewish ones, they got the power. Or they had to serve also in our ghetto. So we lost our independence, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the head of the police, the inside police, was Frankenberg. The police-- the police of the German ghetto, the chief of the police in the German ghetto was [? Haar, ?] but the police of both ghettos was Frankenberg. And later when I went to Buchenwald, he was killed there by our-- by our inmates, by the-- by the people, by the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they counted him as a traitor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- but mostly in the German ghetto, some people would go to the German ghetto, you know. The people in the small ghetto, they were adults, men. And they lost their families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them weren't married yet. And there were again, women in the German ghetto. So there was some romance. There was, you know, going. There were some got girlfriends there. And you had to have a special-- special [? allowness, ?] you know, from-- a permission from the police, the inside police, to go to the German ghetto. But a lot of people would go and meet each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met them at-- we-- sometimes we would work together. Sometimes you would meet them at-- in the ghetto. And a lot of support was from the Latvian-Jewish, for the German, Czech-- Czech-- Czechoslovakian-Jewish and Austrian-Jewish. Because they didn't have-- they had that German language. But they didn't have the Latvian language. And they weren't familiar with the situation in Latvia, in Riga. The Jewish-- the Latvian-Jewish, they knew better, you know, the Latvian language. So-- so they could be more organized. They could get some more food or whatever, when they need it, from the people who were outside or barter something. And then they would help the German-Jewish. Especially-- especially the first time, when they arrived. A lot of support.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything else you'd like to tell me about to ghetto? About the governing council or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a-- there were a-- in to ghetto was an [NON-ENGLISH], they called it. It was people from the Jewish people who would organize a job, where you go to work-- workplace you-- you go. There were-- two of them were working there. And there was-- the-- the elder of the-- of the-- of the-- of the small ghetto, the elder of the small ghetto was [? Max ?] [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. He was a German refugee in Riga. He came to Riga before the war began, as a refugee. And he became the head of the Riga ghetto, the elder of the Riga-- of the small ghetto. And during-- during the killing of the police, of the Latvian police, of the inside Latvian-Jewish police, he played not a very nice role. And people were-- were suspicious. And he participated in that case. And before the ghetto was liquidated, the small ghetto, he was found killed in a cellar. It was the end of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was  the inside and the police, they police a lot-- and police who were only till they got killed. And then we had the German-Jewish police. How long did you stay in the ghetto, till the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the ghetto till-- till beginning of the summer of 1943. And then we were sent for the peat bogs, to [INAUDIBLE], our group. There were different-- we did different parts of Latvia, that people from the ghetto were sent. We were sent to [INAUDIBLE]. They were a small group, about 50 people. And we would work there. Hard work. But it was better than in the ghetto. And we were-- the whole summer we were there. And then as part of us, they took to the farm to dig potatoes and other stuff. And we where there till September, or end of September, not-- till September, September 1943. And then they took us back to the ghetto. And we arrived in the ghetto, the ghetto was almost empty. Almost empty. We didn't know what was going on there, when we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saw that there was no people more, in the ghetto. And then we were told that the people were-- were taken to Kaiserwald. They will build a new camp, a labor camp in Kaiserwald, a nice suburb of Riga.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was close. It was convenient for them because there was an empty place, an empty field. And it was close to the railroad. So they could make the transport. They could bring people, send people. And they build-- the Jewish people build by themselves under the supervise of the Germans, sure. But we were working on to build, what, this camp. And the camp, we were sent to Kaiserwald. After we arrived in the small ghetto, we were almost the last ones from the ghetto. And then we were sent to Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The uncle was already there in Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met him there when we-- I arrived in Kaiserwald. I met him there. So we were taken to Kaiserwald in the end of September. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --it was a terrible experience when we moved. We learned already, in the first night, what it means, Kaiserwald. It was a lot worse, even than the ghetto. It was a-- a concentration camp. Officially it wasn't an-- terminal-- end-- a termination camp. But people would die by themselves without to get to the gas chambers. We didn't have a gas chamber. But people would die constantly there, because of the conditions there. When we came to Kaiserwald, the first night when we arrived at Kaiserwald, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two lines of German guards. And inside, the kapos, they called-- they called them kapos. I mean, it was the professional criminals from German jails they took to supervise, or to direct [? this, ?] for the Jewish people, inside the camp. And sometimes they were worse than the German guards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the first night we had beatings and killing again. And we get stripped naked. They checked us for-- for lice. And we were shaved, taken to the showers.

speaker: interviewee
text: All this stuff was going on by screaming. There was dark night, screaming, yelling, beating. Taken to the shower, the shower, it was a fast enough shower, so it was the dripping the water, cold water was dripping. And then they gave us some clothes, some [? lived-in ?] clothes, so they give us some clothes, whatever it was small or-- or-- or big. And they took us to the barracks. And the barracks they called it-- every barrack, it was called the block. They took us to the block. The block has an elder, block elder. The block elder was a kapo, one. Or he was from the German professional criminals, or there were Pole-- Poles, also criminals. And it was empty yet. The barrack was empty. We had only straw sacks, sacks that was on this-- on the bare soil. We were sleeping there. And in the morning they took us, chased us out for work in the camp the first morning. And we were shoveling there, straighten out the ground, working on the field there, on-- in the camp. And I was also working there, shoveling. And they came, the administration of the camp, they were-- the commandant was [? Al-- ?] [? Albert ?] [? Sauer, ?] Obersturmfuhrer. And he came and he-- he came and stopped where I was working. And he said to me-- screamed to me, he didn't talk. And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll show you how you have to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took my-- the shovel from me and began for a few minutes to shovel, fast, fast. And then he asked, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Did you see how we have to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he put, with the shovel over my back. And he left. People were surprised that he didn't kill me. Because there was many, many cases, such cases, when he killed the man on the spot. This time, I don't know why, but he-- he treat me with a blow with the-- with the shovel and he went. He left. And after that we were taking-- from the same group, we were taking six people to load the freight car that was standing on the railroad. And we were carrying boxes with something inside, we didn't know what that was, to the freight cars. And later as we learned, they-- they-- they were loaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was prepared for people, from the same group. All the group who came with us was taken to-- on the-- the same freight car and taken to Estonia, in some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in some quills? Quails? Or a stone-- I don't know, they're called quarrels, I think. Quarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarries. Yeah, to some quarries for minerals. Some extra location we're going on. We didn't know what was there. But nobody of them showed up, returned. They were-- all of them were killed. So we six, actually, it saved our life in this moment, because we were loading the freight car. I was crying loading it, because it was so hard. It was far. To go from the camp we had to go toe the-- by foot to the railroad and schlep-- carry all the, all the stuff. And all of a sudden and-- and come back and again and again, the whole day. But at this moment, it saved our life. And we remained in Kaiserwald. After that we would work, go to work. I was working in an organization [INAUDIBLE]. It was an engineer corps of the-- of the Germans who supplied-- who worked for the German Army of other-- make other constructions or installations. And they would build-- not far from Kaiserwald, they built a camp for the Polish laborers, forced laborers. And we build the barracks and some bomb shelters, prepare for them. So we were taken to the-- to this place every morning. And in the evening we would return back. When we would come back, there was hard work there. We would come back, we would get a little soup. It was water, some water. And then we were chased again to work in the camp, to build the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would load gravel or sand or-- with the lorries, to-- to-- to push the lorries, to work. We would work all evening long. And then to the barracks, and in the morning again, to work. So was life in Kaiserwald. But it was dull. Every day in Kaiserwald, we had something new.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the time-- it-- it-- every-- something happened, you know, from the guards, also from the kapos inside-- inside the camp. And one night, when I came back, you know, you-- you tried to organize something where you worked to get a piece of bread. And then you would risk with your life, take it to the camp and barter it for something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- once I did-- did it. And I got a little fat. But I couldn't handle it, because I was unused-- you know, I-- for a long time, I didn't use fats or oil.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got the-- the dysentery. And I had to go to the toilet. And then I have an experience. If I would-- I wrote my memories, what happened in Kaiserwald, a few memories in the book, what was published by the Jewish Survivors of Latvia. And maybe if I can, I'll read only a small excerpt of it about this case, what happened with the block eldest. His name was [? Filsinger. ?] He came in, in the toilet and I was there. It was a filthy place where you couldn't even not only sit, but stand down. And you were glad that you didn't fall in, in the hole there, in the peat. And I stood there. It was dark. And-- and a flash flood, you know, came in. I was so scared to death. And he screamed, come with me. And he took me to the block where he the elder. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Without a word, [? Filsinger ?] took a long, rubber-coated stick and started flogging me. I gritted my teeth, determined not to cry out. In my head I counted, one, two, three, four. After each blow, my head jerked to the side. I hoped that my punishment would be no more than five lashes, but my silence infuriated him, and it went on and on. And with every blow, the stick came down harder. He screamed at me to do the counting out loud. And again, I hoped that he would stop at 10. No luck. 11, 12, I was in agony. 13, maybe he would stop at 15. But it was not enough for this tyrant. 16. I started thinking that 20 lashes would be in-- inevitable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gathered all my strength not to show my pain, my shame, or my helplessness. [? Filsinger ?] tried to break me. I did not give in. But my-- but my counting became louder. Then when the 21st blow came down on me, I asked myself, will I hold out until 25?

speaker: interviewee
text: My body was on fire. But this criminal, sadistic man wanted show his supremacy over a truculent 16-years-old Jewish boy who did not want to show his pain and defeat to that bastard in whose hands his fate was placed. I screamed out 25, afraid of losing consciousness. For a moment, there was silence. Then a scream from Filsinger. This is going to happen to each and every one of you who dares to stand up with his feet on the toilet. Then a command to me. Get dressed and come to my room. I pulled my feet out from under the table, bleeding profusely. I straightened up, put on my pants, and trying not to show any sign of weakness, I proceeded to go to the room of my tormentor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, he told me-- he ask me, why did you do it? [? I ?] I announced yesterday that nobody has to stay on the toilet. I said, I'm not from your block. I am from block number one-- from number three, and your block is number one. So he began to laugh, and he said, well, tough luck. [NON-ENGLISH].'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was the case with [? Filsinger ?] when I got 25 lashes. There was other cases, what I-- so what I described in the-- in these memories. Many cases.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is another one, what took place in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --another night. It was in-- in winter of '43, '44, in the winter. It was a strong winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'And I was awaked by a big commotion. What happened? Why is everyone running? I asked [? Faivke.' ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Faivke ?] was my friend, who I had befriended. We were working in the small ghetto, together, and lived at the school, where we worked. And I was with him together. He was with his father. And we were together all the time. 'He said to me, 'don't ask questions,' was his reply. 'Jump down from your bunk quickly, before you get beaten to a pulp.' I heard wild screams, [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. People were running and pushing each through the narrow door. Block Elder [? Mikulas ?] stood there and swung his rubber truncheon right and left, right and left. One of the clocked-- of the block's elder-- orderlies, the Stubendienst Rotbart, pushed everyone out. [? Mikulas ?] had ordered that every man had to run half naked to the bathhouse and get washed. It was a frosty night. Only the little lamps above each block shed faint light. Chased and harassed, half asleep, we behaved like robots in order to avoid being beaten. On days like this, my mother comes to my mind, my warm home. She wakes me up gently. In her sweet voice she tells me, it's time to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: A warm breakfast is waiting for me on the table. I wonder, are there still children in the world who have mothers, who live a normal life just like we did only a few short years ago? Does anyone know what is happening here? Does anyone care? And what is to be our end? How much more can a man endure?'

speaker: interviewee
text: And therefore, [? then, ?] so he came back. And he send me back. We were standing naked outside in the frost. And-- so such-- such events, such-- had happened very often there. There is many more, what I described in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here for a minute. All right, that was good, that you--
